summary: Jeff Atwood presents his 2010 everyday carry (EDC) kit, comparing himself to Batman preparing his utility belt before leaving the house. He details four carefully selected pocket items: a fast 32GB USB drive, a Leatherman Squirt P4 multitool with pliers, a variable-brightness LED flashlight, and a small carabiner. However, the real focus is on how smartphones are rapidly consolidating all these gadgets into one device, replacing everything from cameras to GPS to gaming devices. Atwood marvels at the convergence trend and half-jokingly predicts Apple might add a flip-out blade to future iPhones to complete the takeover.
key_points: The transition from 512MB to 32GB USB drives in just five years represents remarkable technological progress
Pliers are far more useful than scissors on a multitool - a lesson learned from experience
LED flashlight brightness has progressed from bright to 'ridiculously blinding laser-like bright' in five years
Resist the urge to put everything on carabiners - only attach items you actually remove frequently
Smartphones are the ultimate convergence device, already replacing over a dozen separate gadgets
money_quotes: Like any self-respecting geek, I'm mostly an indoor enthusiast.
Now you can have a whole freakin' hard drive worth of files in your pocket. Just in case, you know, you have an emergency need to upload a virus to an alien mothership, or something.
After seeing how much more generally useful the pliers are, I am now a little ashamed to admit I ever carried the wussy scissors version.
Rest assured, everything here is carefully selected with the appropriate levels of monomaniacal attention to detail.
What self-respecting superhero would leave the house these days without their smartphone?
Smartphones really are the ultimate gadget.
At this rate, Apple could introduce a flip-out blade on the iPhone 7 and reduce my entire EDC kit to one item.
key_insight: The smartphone represents the ultimate convergence device, steadily absorbing the functions of nearly every gadget a geek might carry, making dedicated tools increasingly optional.
